notes: Chef review wave 7. Backstage source notes only; public pages should remain recipe-first.

recipes.review_summary: Lakh was broadly sound, but this pass makes the millet-plus-fermented-milk identity clearer and adds common coconut, nutmeg, and baobab flour notes without forcing them.
recommended_changes: Use millet couscous or arraw as the base.
Pair it with sweetened fermented milk, yogurt, or curds.
Keep coconut, nutmeg, vanilla, and baobab powder optional.
recipes.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. West African and Mauritanian references agree on sweet millet porridge or couscous served with fermented milk or yogurt.

sources.title: Wikimedia Commons - Lakh fermented milk topping

recipe.description: Mauritanian-style lakh, soft millet couscous served with sweet fermented milk, yogurt, coconut, and warm spice.
recipe.story: Lakh should feel cool, creamy, and lightly tangy against the nutty millet. Let the grains cool before the milk goes on so the sauce stays fresh.
recipe.best_served_with: Mint tea, dates, roasted peanuts, or fresh fruit

replace_ingredients.name: fermented milk, lait caille, or plain yogurt

replace_steps.title: Steam millet
replace_steps.instruction: Steam the millet couscous over simmering water until the grains are tender and fluffy.

replace_steps.title: Cool and fluff
replace_steps.instruction: Spread the millet in a shallow bowl, sprinkle with salt, and fluff gently so it cools without clumping.

replace_steps.title: Sweeten milk
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk fermented milk or yogurt with sugar, coconut, vanilla, nutmeg, and baobab powder if using.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Spoon the cool millet into bowls and pour the sweet milk sauce over the top just before eating.

recipes.review_summary: The Mauritanian fish rice was close, but it needed the herb stuffing, more vegetable range, and the one-pot tomato broth sequence that makes thieboudienne work.
recommended_changes: Stuff or rub the fish with parsley, garlic, chile, and lemon.
Use tomato paste, onion, cabbage, carrot, eggplant, okra, and root vegetables where available.
Cook the rice in the strained fish-and-vegetable broth.
recipes.official_source_status: Mauritania has regional versions of the wider thieboudienne family. Sources agree on fish, rice, tomato, vegetables, herb seasoning, and communal platter service.

recipe.description: Mauritanian fish rice with tomato-rich broth, fried fish, cabbage, carrots, eggplant, okra, and rice cooked in the sauce.
recipe.story: Good thieboudienne is built in layers: season the fish, cook the vegetables in the broth, then let the rice absorb everything left in the pot.
recipe.best_served_with: Chile paste, lime wedges, cucumber salad, or extra fried fish

replace_ingredients.name: firm ocean fish
replace_ingredients.prep_note: cleaned, scored, and cut in large pieces

replace_steps.title: Season fish
replace_steps.instruction: Pound parsley, garlic, chile, lemon juice, salt, and pepper. Rub it into the scored fish and rest briefly.

replace_steps.title: Brown fish
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a wide pot and brown the fish on both sides. Remove it before it flakes apart.

replace_steps.title: Build broth
replace_steps.instruction: Cook onions, tomato paste, and chopped tomatoes until the paste darkens. Add water or fish stock.

replace_steps.title: Cook vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: Add carrots, cabbage, eggplant, okra, and sweet potato or cassava if using. Simmer until just tender, then lift out with the fish.

replace_steps.title: Cook rice
replace_steps.instruction: Stir rinsed rice into the seasoned broth. Cover and cook low until the rice absorbs the sauce.

replace_steps.title: Assemble platter
replace_steps.instruction: Mound rice on a platter and arrange fish and vegetables over the top. Serve with chile paste or lemon.

recipes.review_summary: Mechoui was sound but too spice-heavy for the usual lamb-roast idea. This pass keeps cumin and butter while making the roasting/resting method clearer.
recommended_changes: Use lamb leg or shoulder when a whole lamb is not practical.
Keep seasoning simple with butter, cumin, garlic, paprika, salt, and pepper.
Roast slowly, then crisp and rest before tearing or carving.
recipes.official_source_status: No single Mauritanian recipe standard found. Country guides and regional references describe mechoui as an important whole-roast lamb or sheep dish.

recipe.description: Mauritanian-style mechoui, slow-roasted lamb rubbed with butter, cumin, garlic, and paprika until tender enough to pull apart.
recipe.story: Mechoui is celebration lamb. The magic is not a crowded spice drawer; it is patience, generous seasoning, and a final blast of heat for the edges.
recipe.best_served_with: Flatbread, couscous, salad, roasted onions, or mint tea

replace_steps.title: Make rub
replace_steps.instruction: Mix butter, cumin, paprika, garlic, coriander if using, salt, and pepper into a thick paste.

replace_steps.title: Season lamb
replace_steps.instruction: Score the lamb and rub the paste deeply into the cuts and over the surface. Rest at room temperature briefly or chill for a longer marinade.

replace_steps.title: Slow roast
replace_steps.instruction: Roast covered at 150C until the lamb is very tender, basting occasionally with pan juices.

replace_steps.title: Crisp outside
replace_steps.instruction: Uncover, raise the oven to 220C, and roast until the surface browns and crackles at the edges.

replace_steps.title: Rest and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Rest the lamb before carving or tearing. Serve with bread, couscous, salad, and lemon if using.

recipes.review_summary: Dholl puri was strong. This pass adds the reserved split-pea cooking water, finer yellow-pea crumb, and classic gros pois, rougaille, chutney, and achard serving set.
recommended_changes: Cook split peas until crushable but not wet.
Use the pea cooking water to soften the dough.
Serve thin, folded, and filled with bean curry, rougaille, chutney, and achard.
recipes.official_source_status: No official standard found. Mauritian street-food sources strongly agree on yellow split peas, turmeric, thin griddle flatbread, and curry/chutney fillings.

recipe.description: Mauritian dholl puri, thin yellow-split-pea flatbread folded around butter bean curry, rougaille, chutney, and achard.
recipe.story: The beauty is in the thinness. Grind the peas fine, keep the dough soft, and roll gently so the filling spreads without tearing.
recipe.best_served_with: Butter bean curry, rougaille, coriander chutney, achard, and chili paste

recipes.review_summary: Gateau piment was already close. This pass keeps the split-pea fritter structure and sharpens the street-food cues with curry leaves, spring onion, coriander, and baguette service.
recommended_changes: Use soaked yellow split peas ground coarsely.
Season with chile, onion, coriander, spring onion, cumin, and curry leaves where available.
Fry small and serve hot, plain or inside bread.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Mauritian recipes consistently describe yellow-split-pea chilli fritters fried until crisp.

sources.title: The Foodie's Avenue - Mauritian Gateau Piment
Royal Curry Club - Rougaille Gateaux Piment

recipe.description: Mauritian gateau piment, crisp chilli fritters made from soaked yellow split peas, herbs, cumin, and green chile.
recipe.story: Do not puree the peas into baby food. A coarse paste gives gateau piment its crisp shell and tender, grainy middle.
recipe.best_served_with: Fresh baguette, butter, coriander chutney, or rougaille

replace_steps.title: Grind peas
replace_steps.instruction: Drain soaked split peas well and grind to a coarse paste, scraping down the bowl so some texture remains.

replace_steps.title: Season paste
replace_steps.instruction: Mix in onion, chilies, garlic, coriander, spring onions, cumin, curry leaves if using, baking soda, and salt.

replace_steps.title: Shape fritters
replace_steps.instruction: With damp hands, shape small balls or rough patties so they fry evenly.

replace_steps.title: Fry crisp
replace_steps.instruction: Fry in hot oil until deeply golden and crisp outside, turning as needed.

replace_steps.title: Serve hot
replace_steps.instruction: Drain well and serve immediately, plain or tucked into bread with chutney or rougaille.

recipes.review_summary: Rougaille needed a more flexible Mauritian sausage note. This pass keeps the tomato-thyme-garlic-ginger sauce and allows pork, chicken, beef, or smoked sausages.
recommended_changes: Use Mauritian-style sausage, smoked sausage, pork, chicken, or beef sausage.
Cook tomato sauce down until thick, not watery.
Finish with herbs and serve with rice, dal, or dholl puri.
recipes.official_source_status: No official standard found. Mauritian references agree on tomato-based rougaille with onion, garlic, ginger, thyme, chile, and sausage or seafood variants.

recipe.description: Mauritian sausage rougaille, a thick tomato, thyme, garlic, ginger, and chile sauce simmered with browned sausages.
recipe.story: Rougaille should taste bright and cooked down, not raw tomato. Let the tomatoes collapse before the sausage goes back in.
recipe.best_served_with: Steamed rice, lentils, dholl puri, cucumber salad, or achard

replace_ingredients.name: Mauritian, smoked, pork, chicken, or beef sausages

replace_steps.title: Brown sausage
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and brown the sausage slices on both sides. Remove them so they do not overcook.

replace_steps.title: Cook aromatics
replace_steps.instruction: Cook onions in the same pot until soft, then add garlic, ginger, thyme, and curry leaves if using.

replace_steps.title: Reduce tomatoes
replace_steps.instruction: Add tomatoes and whole chile. Cook until the tomatoes collapse into a thick sauce.

replace_steps.title: Simmer sausage
replace_steps.instruction: Return sausage to the pot and simmer gently until the sauce coats every piece.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Remove the whole chile if you want a milder dish. Stir in coriander or parsley and adjust salt.

recipes.review_summary: The tagine was close. This pass adds parsley, olive oil, turmeric, and the bread-friendly serving cue, and removes couscous as the default pairing.
recommended_changes: Use chicken, grated or sliced onion, garlic, ginger, turmeric, saffron, preserved lemon, olives, parsley, and cilantro.
Cook gently until the onion sauce reduces.
Serve with Moroccan bread for scooping.
recipes.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Moroccan and cookbook references consistently describe chicken with preserved lemon, olives, onion, ginger, saffron or turmeric, herbs, and slow cooking.

sources.title: Food Network - Chicken Tagine with Olives and Preserved Lemons
Cooks Without Borders - Anissa Helou Chicken Tagine
Cooking the Globe - Moroccan Chicken Tagine

recipe.description: Moroccan chicken tagine with preserved lemons, olives, saffron, turmeric, onion, parsley, and cilantro.
recipe.story: The sauce should reduce into a glossy onion gravy. Add the preserved lemon and olives late so they perfume the dish without turning bitter.
recipe.best_served_with: Moroccan bread, cucumber salad, mint tea, or simple roasted vegetables

replace_steps.title: Marinate chicken
replace_steps.instruction: Rub chicken with garlic, ginger, turmeric, saffron water if using, salt, pepper, half the herbs, and olive oil.

replace_steps.title: Build tagine
replace_steps.instruction: Spread onions in a tagine or heavy pot. Add chicken and any marinade, then pour in a little water or stock.

replace_steps.title: Cook gently
replace_steps.instruction: Cover and cook over medium-low heat until the chicken is tender and the onion sauce has reduced.

replace_steps.title: Add lemon and olives
replace_steps.instruction: Add preserved lemon rind and olives. Simmer uncovered until the sauce is glossy and concentrated.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Scatter remaining parsley and cilantro over the tagine and serve with bread for scooping.

recipes.review_summary: Harira was basically correct but needed celery, vermicelli, and clearer tadouira-style flour thickening. The update keeps chickpeas, lentils, tomatoes, herbs, spices, and lemon.
recommended_changes: Use chickpeas, lentils, tomato, onion, celery, herbs, ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, and pepper.
Add vermicelli near the end.
Thicken with a flour-water slurry and finish with lemon.
recipes.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Moroccan references consistently describe harira as a tomato, legume, herb, meat, and flour-thickened soup, especially during Ramadan.

recipe.description: Moroccan harira, a Ramadan tomato soup with lamb, chickpeas, lentils, celery, herbs, vermicelli, and lemon.
recipe.story: Harira should be hearty but pourable. Let the legumes soften first, then thicken gently so the soup turns silky instead of pasty.
recipe.best_served_with: Dates, lemon wedges, chebakia, hard-boiled eggs, or crusty bread

replace_ingredients.name: plain flour
replace_ingredients.prep_note: mixed with water into a slurry

replace_steps.title: Brown base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and brown meat if using. Add onion and celery and cook until soft.

replace_steps.title: Spice tomatoes
replace_steps.instruction: Add ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, pepper, tomatoes, cilantro, and parsley. Cook until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Simmer legumes
replace_steps.instruction: Add chickpeas, lentils, and water or stock. Simmer until the chickpeas and lentils are tender.

replace_steps.title: Add noodles
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in vermicelli and cook until tender.

replace_steps.title: Thicken and brighten
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk in the flour slurry gradually and simmer until silky. Finish with lemon juice and adjust salt.

recipes.review_summary: Msemen was good. This pass emphasizes lamination with oil, butter, and semolina, keeps the square fold, and avoids overstating yeast as essential.
recommended_changes: Make a soft flour-semolina dough.
Rest well, stretch thin, oil generously, dust with semolina, and fold into squares.
Cook on a hot griddle and serve with honey, butter, or tea.
recipes.official_source_status: No official standard found. Moroccan references agree on layered square griddle flatbread made from flour, semolina, water, oil, butter, and salt.

recipe.description: Moroccan msemen, flaky square griddle flatbread laminated with oil, butter, and fine semolina.
recipe.story: Msemen is all touch. The dough needs enough rest to stretch paper-thin, and enough oil and semolina to make the layers separate.
recipe.best_served_with: Honey, butter, jam, soft cheese, mint tea, or amlou

replace_steps.title: Knead dough
replace_steps.instruction: Mix flour, semolina, salt, sugar if using, and warm water. Knead until smooth, soft, and elastic.

replace_steps.title: Rest balls
replace_steps.instruction: Divide into small balls, coat with oil, cover, and rest so the dough relaxes.

replace_steps.title: Stretch and fold
replace_steps.instruction: Stretch one ball very thin on an oiled surface. Brush with butter-oil, dust with semolina, and fold into a square.

replace_steps.title: Cook griddle
replace_steps.instruction: Flatten each square gently and cook on a hot griddle until golden spots form and the layers puff.

replace_steps.title: Serve warm
replace_steps.instruction: Serve hot with honey, butter, jam, cheese, or mint tea.

recipes.review_summary: The prawn curry was solid. This pass leans into the Mozambican coconut, tomato, curry spice, garlic, ginger, lime, and piri-piri profile.
recommended_changes: Keep prawns brief-cooked so they stay sweet.
Build sauce from onion, garlic, ginger, tomato, curry powder, turmeric, coconut milk, and piri-piri.
Finish with lime and coriander.
recipes.official_source_status: No single official standard found. Mozambican and Indo-Portuguese references agree on shrimp curry with coconut milk, tomato, aromatics, and curry spices.

recipe.description: Mozambican caril de camarão, prawns simmered briefly in coconut milk, tomato, curry spice, garlic, ginger, and piri-piri.
recipe.story: The sauce can take its time, but the prawns cannot. Build the curry first, then add seafood at the end and stop as soon as it curls.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, xima, lemon wedges, or simple tomato salad

recipes.review_summary: Matapa had a dietary mismatch: it was marked vegan while still allowing shrimp. This pass makes the common seafood version explicit and removes the vegan tag.
recommended_changes: Use pounded cassava leaves, peanuts, coconut milk, garlic, onion, and piri-piri.
Include shrimp, dried shrimp, or crab as the common coastal version.
Cook cassava leaves long enough to soften fully.
recipes.official_source_status: Mozambican references consistently describe matapa as cassava leaves cooked with ground peanuts and coconut milk, commonly seasoned with seafood such as shrimp or crab.

recipe.description: Mozambican matapa, pounded cassava leaves simmered with ground peanuts, coconut milk, garlic, piri-piri, and shrimp.
recipe.story: Matapa should be creamy and earthy, with the cassava leaves cooked past toughness. The seafood seasons the pot rather than taking it over.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, xima, grilled prawns, or piri-piri chicken

replace_ingredients.name: shrimp, dried shrimp, or crab meat

replace_steps.title: Start leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Put pounded cassava leaves, garlic, chile if using, and water in a pot. Simmer until the leaves begin to soften.

replace_steps.title: Add peanut
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in ground peanuts and onion. Cook gently, stirring often so the peanut does not catch.

replace_steps.title: Simmer creamy
replace_steps.instruction: Add coconut milk and continue simmering until the leaves are tender and the sauce is creamy.

replace_steps.title: Cook seafood
replace_steps.instruction: Add shrimp, dried shrimp, or crab and simmer until cooked through and absorbed into the sauce.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Season with salt and lemon juice if using. Serve hot over rice or xima.

recipes.review_summary: Piri piri chicken was ingredient-correct but the page time ignored the important marinade. The update makes the African bird's-eye chile sauce and rest time explicit.
recommended_changes: Use a spatchcocked chicken for even grilling.
Blend piri-piri chiles with garlic, lemon or lime, vinegar, paprika, oil, and salt.
Marinate for at least four hours, then grill and baste.
recipes.official_source_status: No single official standard found. Mozambican and Portuguese-influenced references agree on chicken marinated and basted with chile, garlic, citrus or vinegar, oil, and spice.

sources.title: The Guardian - Perfect Piri Piri Chicken

recipe.description: Mozambican piri piri chicken, spatchcocked and grilled with African bird's-eye chile, garlic, lemon, vinegar, paprika, and oil.
recipe.story: The sauce is both marinade and basting brush. Let it sit with the chicken long enough to stain the meat, then grill until smoky and glossy.
recipe.best_served_with: Matapa, rice, fries, tomato salad, lime wedges, or extra piri-piri sauce

replace_steps.title: Blend sauce
replace_steps.instruction: Blend piri-piri chiles, garlic, lemon juice, roasted pepper, paprika, oregano, vinegar, olive oil, and salt into a punchy sauce.

replace_steps.title: Marinate chicken
replace_steps.instruction: Score the spatchcocked chicken, rub with most of the sauce, and chill for at least four hours or overnight. Reserve some sauce for basting.

replace_steps.title: Grill and baste
replace_steps.instruction: Grill over medium-hot charcoal, turning and basting regularly until the skin is burnished and smoky.

replace_steps.title: Check and rest
replace_steps.instruction: Cook until juices run clear and the thickest part reaches 75C. Rest before cutting so the juices settle.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Cut into pieces and squeeze lime over the top. Serve with rice, fries, matapa, or extra piri-piri sauce.
